original_abstract: Alkali metal-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with permanent porosity are scarce because of their high tendency to coordinate with solvents such as water. However, these MOFs are lightweight and bear gravimetric benefits for gas adsorption related applications. In this study, we present the successful construction of a microporous MOF, designated as HIAM-111, built solely on sodium ions by using an octacarboxylate linker. The structure of HIAM-111 is based on 8-connected Na<sub>4</sub> clusters and exhibits a novel topology with an underlying 3<sup>2</sup>,4<sup>2</sup>,8-c net. Remarkably, HAM-111 possesses a robust and highly porous framework with a BET surface area of 1561 m<sup>2</sup>/g, significantly surpassing that of the previously reported Na-MOFs. Further investigations demonstrate that HIAM-111 is capable of separating C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>2</sub>/CO<sub>2</sub> and purifying C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>4</sub> directly from C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>4</sub>/C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>2</sub>/C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>6</sub> with high adsorption capacities. The current work may shed light on the rational design of robust and porous MOFs based on alkali metals.
humanized_abstract: Most alkali metal-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with permanent porosity tend to dissolve in common solvent like water due to their high solubility, which have limited their development. However, MOFs based on alkali metals still retain some potential because of their low density and large gas adsorption capacity at low weight per unit mass level. Here, we developed a microporous MOF denoted as HIAM-111 through sole use of sodium ions in combination with an octacarboxylate linker. Based on powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies, HIAM-111 was identified to possess a 8-connected Na4 cluster architecture with a new topological structure named as 3^2,4^2,8-c and possessed a robust and highly porous structure featuring a BET surface area of 1561 m2/g which is the largest value compared to other known sodium-based MOFs. It demonstrated excellent performance for C2H2/CO2 separation and more importantly, it showed great capability in purifying C2H4 from mixtures of C2H4/C2H2/C2H6 with high adsorption capacities. This work may open new avenues for the preparation of high stability and high porosity MOFs based on alkali metals.
